name: Ascent to El Yelmo — circular route around the Tranco Reservoir
name_original: Subida a el Yelmo — circular por el Embalse del Tranco
region_intro: Road cycling around Embalse de Tranco de Beas offers routes through mountainous terrain and extensive pine forests within the Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas Natural Park. The region features varied elevations, with the reservoir itself at 621 meters above sea level, providing diverse cycling challenges. Cyclists can find paths that run along the edge of the Tranco reservoir, offering consistent views of the water and surrounding natural beauty. This area is characterized by its rugged landscapes and the presence of the Guadalquivir River, which forms the reservoir. Best no traffic road cycling routes around Embalse de Tranco de Beas The most popular no traffic road cycling route is C.F. El Campillo Shelter – Segura de la Sierra loop from Cementerio Municipal, a 67.1 miles (108.0 km) trail that takes 5 hours 59 minutes to complete. This difficult route features significant elevation gain through the natural park. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is C.F. El Campillo Shelter – El Yelmo Peak loop from Hornos, a difficult 48.5 miles (78.0 km) path. This route includes challenging ascents towards El Yelmo Peak, offering panoramic views of the surrounding mountains and forests. Local road cyclists also love the Beas de Segura – Beas de Segura loop from Cementerio Municipal, a 29.7 miles (47.9 km) trail leading through varied landscapes around the reservoir, often completed in about 2 hours 33 minutes. Road cycling around Embalse de Tranco de Beas is defined by mountainous terrain, pine forests, and waterside paths along the reservoir. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate loops to difficult routes with substantial elevation gain. The routes in Embalse de Tranco de Beas are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.6 stars from 8 reviews. More than 700 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Embalse de Tranco de Beas's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many no traffic road cycling routes are available around Embalse de Tranco de Beas?
A: There are 5 dedicated no traffic road cycling routes available around Embalse de Tranco de Beas, offering a range of experiences from moderate to difficult.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on the no traffic road cycling routes?
A: The routes around Embalse de Tranco de Beas feature a diverse landscape. You'll encounter mountainous terrain with challenging ascents through extensive pine forests, as well as scenic stretches along the edge of the reservoir, offering consistent views of the water. The area's elevation, with the reservoir at 621 meters above sea level, ensures varied and engaging cycling.

Q: Are there any challenging no traffic road cycling routes for experienced riders?
A: Yes, the region offers several challenging routes. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20123360" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;C.F. El Campillo Shelter – El Yelmo Peak loop from Hornos&lt;/a&gt; covers over 78 km with nearly 2000 meters of elevation gain. Another demanding option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40302884" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;C.F. El Campillo Shelter – Segura de la Sierra loop from Cementerio Municipal&lt;/a&gt;, which spans 108 km with over 2300 meters of climbing.

Q: What interesting landmarks or viewpoints can I see along these routes?
A: Many routes offer stunning views and pass by notable landmarks. You can enjoy panoramic vistas from spots like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3896738" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Isla Cabeza de la Viña Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5315084" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Tranco de Beas Reservoir&lt;/a&gt;. The reservoir itself, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5391992" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tranco de Beas Reservoir&lt;/a&gt;, is a central feature, and routes often connect to traditional villages like Hornos de Segura, which retains a medieval character.

Q: When is the best time of year to enjoy traffic-free road cycling in this region?
A: The Embalse de Tranco de Beas area, being in Jaén, Spain, generally offers pleasant cycling conditions for much of the year. Spring and autumn are typically ideal, with milder temperatures and beautiful natural scenery. Summers can be hot, especially in July and August, while winters are cooler but often still suitable for cycling, particularly on sunny days.

Q: Are there any circular no traffic road cycling routes?
A: Yes, all the listed no traffic road cycling routes in this guide are circular, allowing you to start and end at the same point. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31861136" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Beas de Segura – Beas de Segura loop from Cementerio Municipal&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40017317" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Hornos de Segura – Hornos Viewpoint loop from Pontones&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about cycling in Embalse de Tranco de Beas?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.6 out of 5 stars. Reviewers often praise the stunning natural beauty, the challenging climbs, and the serene, traffic-free roads that allow for an immersive cycling experience within the Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas Natural Park.

Q: Is public transport available to reach the Embalse de Tranco de Beas area with a bike?
A: Public transport options directly to the Embalse de Tranco de Beas area, especially those accommodating bicycles, can be limited. It's advisable to check local bus services or consider driving to a starting point with your bike. The region is best accessed by car, allowing flexibility to reach various route starting points.

Q: Are there any specific permits required for cycling within the Sierra de Cazorla Natural Park?
A: Generally, no specific permits are required for road cycling on public roads within the Sierra de Cazorla, Segura y Las Villas Natural Park. However, always respect local regulations, especially concerning protected areas, and stay on designated routes. For specific activities or off-road trails, it's always best to check with the park authorities.

Q: Where can I find parking for these traffic-free road cycling routes?
A: Many of the routes start from or pass through villages like Hornos de Segura or Beas de Segura, where you can typically find public parking areas. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40302884" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;C.F. El Campillo Shelter – Segura de la Sierra loop&lt;/a&gt; starts near a municipal cemetery, which often has parking available. It's recommended to check specific starting points for parking availability before your ride.

Q: Are there any easy or family-friendly no traffic road cycling routes?
A: While the guide focuses on moderate to difficult routes, the region does offer varied terrain. For easier sections, look for routes that stick closer to the reservoir's edge, which tend to have less extreme elevation changes. However, most listed routes include significant climbs, so they are generally better suited for cyclists with some experience. Always check the route's elevation profile for suitability.
tips: Prepare to climb. The road begins to climb as soon as you leave Hornos. Beautiful beginning of the path angling between the trees. The final 5.4km stretch begins as you pass a stone ruin. The difference in altitude is 510m at an average of 6% until reaching El Yelmo at 1780m above sea level.
The Tranco reservoir is one of the largest in Spain.
